title: pi-context update has no specified transactional boundary between the schema loop and the post-loop config-registry propagation: the registry write (index.ts:1391-1421) is ungated on the schema-loop outcome, so a run reporting a schema as blocked still mutates config.json
description: pi-context update applies, in one run, two steps with opposite risk profiles: additive config-registry vocabulary propagation (relation_types/block_kinds/invariants/lenses — non-destructive) and per-schema version migration (the only step that can return blocked). The post-loop registry propagation (packages/pi-context/src/index.ts:1391-1421) calls writeConfig when !dryRun regardless of whether a sibling schema in the same run returned blocked — the registry write is ungated on the schema-loop outcome. So a live update that reports a schema as blocked still mutates config.json with the catalog-new vocabulary. update has no specified transactional boundary across the schema loop and the registry propagation; the per-schema byte-exact rollback (resyncSchema, index.ts:599-641) bounds only the blocked schema's own file and migrations.json, not the registry write.

evidence.reference: post-loop registry propagation computes registryAdditions and calls writeConfig when !dryRun, ungated on the schema-loop outcome — runs even when a sibling schema is in result.blocked
resyncSchema per-schema byte-exact rollback bounds only the blocked schema's file + migrations.json declarations, not the registry write
R-0012 — live reproduction: a schema returned blocked while registryAdditions were written to config.json in the same run

impact: A caller of update cannot assume a run that reports a schema as blocked left config.json unchanged: the additive vocabulary lands regardless. Without a specified transactional boundary the run's effect on config is implicit and undocumented, and a blocked result reads as a no-op while config was mutated.
proposed_resolution: Specify update's transactional boundary across the schema loop and the config-registry propagation — atomic (any block rolls back the registry write and every schema together) vs per-component (each component applies independently: the blocked schema rolls back byte-exact, sibling schemas and the additive registry propagation proceed). Per-component extends DEC-0017's sibling-schema independence to the registry-propagation seam.
